text: If you want space for the rest of this shower so we can figure out what we are later...then I’ll give you space.” He steps back and I instantly feel the loss of him, my anxiety ratcheting up even higher with the growing distance between us. He crosses his arms expectantly, and my mind begins to spin with all the possible scenarios at play here. Everyone here knows me as Trista the Surrogate. Trista the Incubator having the grumpy mountain man’s baby. Now I’m Trista the... Butterflies erupt in my belly over the new potential labels I could give myself. I could give us. The possibilities give me a new perspective on life, one that I barely let myself consider before. I lick my lips and smile up at Mr. Mountain Man. His trim beard, his fresh button-down, his grin—it all looks divinely good on him. Stepping forward, I reach out and take his hand in mine. “No space, please.” I tip my head back and offer my lips to him, and he kindly obliges. I hear a faint gasp and murmurings coming from all around us, and I decide to go full send and wrap my hands around his neck to deepen the kiss. His husky laugh tastes like the hope that I’d scarcely allowed myself to have in my life. As we join the party, I gird my fruitful loins for some harsh judgment. There might even be some looks of disgust...a surrogate and her intended parent outing their relationship isn’t exactly conventional. But as I look from face to face, all I see are smiles. Genuine, knowing and maybe even somewhat smug smiles. His mother even winks at me. They all knew. Every damn one of them. I don’t know if Wyatt told them or if we weren’t as good at keeping our secret as I thought. But this family I found out here doesn’t seem the least bit surprised by our change in relationship status. A loud throat-clearing has Wyatt and I separating from another kiss with silly, dopey smiles. I look over and see Calder standing there with a skeptical expression on his face. “Does this mean I can start calling you Mama Bear?” His brows waggle knowingly. My heart lurches as I press my hand to my belly and turn toward my mountain man. “I might insist on it.” Chapter 45 Weeks Pregnant: 30 Animals on the Mountain: 10 Wyatt “I’ll get the rest,” I state to Calder as I squat and lift the three last boxes. “You guys go ahead.” My brothers nod and make their way down the steps and through the barn to carry the final load of Trista’s belonging to my place. “I’m getting a strange sense of déjà vu,” Trista says as she bends over to pick up a laundry basket filled with kitchen gadgets. I clear my throat loudly and she turns on her heel to eye me with that challenging twinkle. “You weren’t just about to pick that up, were you?” I narrow my eyes on her and set the boxes I’m holding down on the table. “Yes, I was,” she replies with a laugh. “It’s not that heavy.” “It’s also not necessary for you to pick up when I’m right here.” I stalk slowly towards the woman carrying my child, my eyes drinking in every growing inch of her with great satisfaction. She’s never looked more beautiful than she does right now, that I can’t get any luckier, but I’m always proven wrong the next day when she dares to look even more beautiful. “I shouldn’t have to remind you that you’re carrying precious cargo.” I cup her growing belly possessively, an act that sends a surge of pride through me every time. How I could have gone through our whole pregnancy without holding her like this...feeling the baby kick...watching her eyes light up every time she has a moment with the baby all on her own...it’s a sad, scary thought. And to watch her embrace the pregnancy more fully these past several weeks is a beautiful sight too. I respected her decision in the beginning to give it up and truly just be a surrogate. It’s a gift not many people can do. But I respect her even more for knowing her own mind and advocating for what she wanted. And if sharing custody was all she wanted out of me, I would have accepted that. It would have killed me because I think I’d been in love with this woman far longer than I realized, but I would have done what was right. But to sit here, holding her, loving her, and moving her into my place and planning a whole damn life with her. I don’t know what I did to deserve this. I half wonder if my dad is pulling strings up above because this is everything he ever wanted for me. Trista smirks defiantly up at me as she clutches my hands on her belly. “My therapist says my willful independence is called avoidant attachment disorder because of all my abandonment issues as a child. You want to unpack that further with me?” My lip’s part as my heart sinks in my chest. “Hey Lucky, I’m sorry I didn’t mean—” “Don’t be sorry.” She presses her hand to my lips to stop my yammering. “It helps for me to make light of what I’m learning about myself in therapy. It’s all a part of the work I’m putting in to not pass my fuckedupness down to our baby.” A sad smile tugs at the corner of my mouth. She started therapy almost immediately after the baby shower. She said she needed to work through this embracing motherhood stuff on her own and unpack years of childhood shit that I only know the highlights of. All I know is that if ever there is a day her parents try to walk back into her life, it will be one where I’ll need
